1: מַעֲנֶה רַּךְ (maʿăneh rak, a soft/gentle answer) — maʿăneh is the response that meets a question or situation; rak is soft, tender. The verb yāšîb ḥēmâ (turns away wrath) uses the return/restore verb: the soft answer sends wrath back the way it came. A grievous word (debar-ʿeṣeb, a word of pain/toil) stirs up (yaʿăleh, causes to rise) wrath — the uprising image of anger inflamed.
2: לְשׁוֹן חֲכָמִים (tongue of the wise) תֵּיטִיב דָּעַת (presents knowledge rightly/makes knowledge good) — the Hiphil of yāṭab: causes knowledge to be good, presents it well. The mouth of fools (pî כְּסִילִים) yabbîaʿ (gushes/pours out) foolishness — the same word used for springs and fountains overflowing; uncontrolled output.
3: עֵינֵי יהוה (eyes of the LORD) are in every place (bəkol-māqôm) — omnipresence stated plainly. They watch (ṣōpôt, Qal participle: constantly surveying) both the evil and the good. The theological import: there is no hidden space. The verse functions as the epistemological foundation for the entire Proverbs moral system — behavior is always observed.
4: מַרְפֵּא לָשׁוֹן (a healing tongue) is a ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) — the Genesis phrase again (cf. Proverbs 13:12). The tongue as a tree of life: speech as a life-giving or life-denying force. Selep (crookedness/perverseness) in it is a breaking of the spirit (šeber bərûaḥ) — the same šābar used for the breaking of bones and the fracture of nations; twisted speech destroys at that depth.
5: אֱוִיל (ʾĕwîl) — one of several Hebrew words for fool; this one emphasizes moral stubbornness rather than mere ignorance. He despises (yinʾaṣ, Qal: holds in contempt) his father's mûsār (discipline). The one who guards (šōmēr, keeps watch over) reproof (tôkaḥat) shows good sense (yaʿrim, acts prudently/craftily in the good sense).
6: בֵּית צַדִּיק חֹסֶן רָב (house of the righteous is great treasure) — ḥōsen is the word for stored wealth or treasury; the household is a wealth-store not just materially but structurally. The income of the wicked (tebûʾat rāšāʿ) brings ʿăkārâ (trouble/disturbance) — the word used for Achan's troubling of Israel (Joshua 7:25).
7: שִׂפְתֵי חֲכָמִים (lips of the wise) יְזָרוּ דָעַת (spread/scatter knowledge) — the verb zārâ is used for winnowing grain; wisdom spread by speech is like grain scattered in the wind, broadcast generously. The heart (lēb) of fools — not their mouth — is not so (right/established) — the problem begins deeper than speech.
8: זֶבַח רְשָׁעִים תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה (sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination of the LORD) — tôʿăbâ is the cultic revulsion-word: what is fundamentally incompatible with God's holiness, specifically what violates covenant relationship. Sacrifice without inner righteousness is not merely ineffective; it is actively offensive. Tephilat yəšārîm (prayer of the upright) is his raṣôn (delight/pleasure/favor).
9: The double use of תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה in Proverbs 15:8–9 (sacrifice, then the way/lifestyle of the wicked) intensifies: not just cultic performance but life-orientation provokes divine revulsion. God yəʾahab (loves, Piel: deeply loves) whoever earnestly pursues (məraddēp, Piel participle: continuously, hotly pursues) righteousness — the verb suggests relentless chase.
10: מוּסָר רָע (evil/harsh discipline) for the one who ʿōzēb ʾōraḥ (forsakes the path/way) — the abandonment of the way earns painful discipline. Śōnēʾ tôkaḥat (hater of reproof) will die — the imperfect is stark: this is the trajectory. The connection is not mechanical punishment but the internal logic of rejecting the corrective that sustains life.
11: שְׁאוֹל וַאֲבַדּוֹן (Šəʾôl wəʾăbaddôn) — the paired names for the realm of death; Abaddon means destruction/perdition (from ʾābad, to perish). Both "lie open (nəgêd, before/visible) to the LORD" — the underworld is not hidden from divine sight. The rhetorical ʾap kî (how much more): if even death's domain is transparent to God, how much more are human hearts.
12: לֵץ לֹא-יֶאֱהַב (the mocker does not love) being reproved (hōkêaḥ lô) — the same ʾāhab (love) as genuine attachment; the mocker's aversion to correction is visceral and defining. He will not go (wəʾel-ḥăkāmîm lōʾ yēlēk) to the company of the wise — self-isolation from the corrective community is the mocker's chosen condition.
13: לֵב שָׂמֵחַ (a joyful/glad heart) makes the face (pānîm) good (yêṭib, Hiphil: causes the face to be good) — the inward state shows outwardly. A crushed spirit (bəʿaṣbat-rûaḥ) — ʿaṣab is pain/grief/toil — breaks/bows (nəkēʾâ rûaḥ) the spirit. The psychosomatic connection is explicit in both directions: inner peace radiates outward; inner grief collapses inward.
14: לֵב נָבוֹן (a discerning heart) is always seeking (yəbaqquēš, Piel: continuously, intensively seeks) knowledge (dāʿat). The mouth of fools (pî kəsîlîm) feeds (yirʿeh, literally grazes/shepherds) on folly — the pastoral metaphor is precise: fools graze on foolishness as naturally as sheep on grass. The contrast is active seeking vs. passive consumption of one's default diet.
15: כָּל-יְמֵי עָנִי רָעִים (all the days of the afflicted are evil) — ʿānî is the poor/afflicted/oppressed person; their subjective experience of daily life is consistently bad. But a good heart (ṭôb-lēb) is a continual feast (mišteh tāmîd) — mišteh is a banquet/drinking feast; the contrast is between the bleakness of external poverty and the inner abundance of the joyful heart. The inner life can override external conditions.
16: בְּיִרְאַת יהוה (with the fear of the LORD) accompanying little (məʿaṭ) is better (ṭôb) than great treasure (ʾôṣār rāb) with turmoil (ûmehûmâ bô) — mehûmâ is the panic and tumult of battle or disaster; wealth without divine orientation generates its own chaos. The ṭôb (better-than) formula is a wisdom rhetorical device for relative evaluation.
17: אֲרֻחַת יָרָק (a portion of herbs/vegetables) with love (ʾahăbâ šām, love being there) is better than šôr ʾăbûs (a fattened ox) with hatred (śinʾâ bô). The ʾăbûs (stall-fed) ox is the luxury meal. The relational quality of the meal determines its nourishment more than the food itself.
18: אִישׁ חֵמָה (a man of wrath/heat) stirs up (yəgāreh, Piel: provokes, picks a fight with) strife (māḏôn). The one slow to anger (ʾerek ʾapayyim, long of nostrils — the same idiom as Proverbs 14:29) quiets (yəšakkēn, Piel: causes to dwell/settle) strife. The Piel in both cases intensifies: the provocation is deliberate, and the quieting is active work.
19: דֶּרֶךְ עָצֵל (way of the lazy) is like a hedge of thorns (kəמְשׂוּכַת קוֹצִים) — məśûkâ is a barrier of thorns, an impenetrable wall. The road of the upright (ʾōraḥ yəšārîm) is a level highway (səlullâ, a built-up road, the kind paved for royal procession). The contrast is not just easy vs. hard but organic obstacle vs. constructed path.
20: בֵּן חָכָם (a wise son) makes his father glad (yəśammēaḥ-ʾāb, Piel: causes joy to his father). A foolish man (ûkəsîl אָדָם) despises his mother (bôzeh ʾimmô) — the contempt word. The shift from "son" to "man" in the second half is deliberate: the grown fool, who should have matured, still acts like the scorner of ch. Proverbs 13:1.
21: אִוֶּלֶת שִׂמְחָה לַחֲסַר-לֵב (folly is joy/entertainment to the one lacking heart/understanding) — ḥăsar-lēb (lacking heart) is the Proverbs phrase for the person without moral judgment; folly actually brings them pleasure. The person of understanding (ʾîš tebûnâ) walks straight (yəyaššer-lāket) — the causative of yāšar: makes his walking straight, corrects his own course.
22: הֲפֵר מַחֲשָׁבוֹת (plans come to nothing/are frustrated) where there is no counsel (bəʾên sôd) — sôd is the intimate counsel of a close group, the divine council language used in Amos 3:7 and Jeremiah 23:18. With the multitude of advisors (bərōb yôʿăṣîm) plans are established (tāqûm, will stand/arise). The wisdom of collaborative discernment echoes the divine council pattern.
23: שִׂמְחָה לָאִישׁ בְּמַעֲנֵה-פִיו (joy belongs to a person in the answer of his mouth) — the joy of giving the right word; maʿăneh (answer/response) is the same root as Proverbs 15:1. The rhetorical question ûדָבָר בְּעִתּוֹ מַה-טּוֹב (a word in its time, how good it is!) — ʿittô (its time, the right moment): timing is as important as content.
24: אֹרַח חַיִּים לְמַעְלָה (the path of life leads upward) — ləmaʿlâ is a spatial-moral indicator: upward, heavenward. The purpose clause: לְמַעַן סוּר מִשְּׁאוֹל מָטָּה (in order to turn from Sheol below) — the spatial antithesis is complete. The wise person's life-trajectory is upward, away from the downward pull toward the realm of death.
25: בֵּית גֵּאִים (house of the proud) will be torn down (yissaḥ, Qal: uprooted/pulled apart) by the LORD — direct divine action. The widow's property line (גְּבוּל אַלְמָנָה) he maintains (yaṣṣēb, Hiphil: causes to stand, maintains firmly). The widow as the paradigmatic vulnerable person in Israelite law; the LORD as her boundary-keeper against the encroachment of the powerful.
26: The third use of תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה in chapter 15 (Proverbs 15:8, 9, 26) — now applied to the plans (maḥšəbôt) of the wicked. Pure words (ʾimrê-ṭōhar, words of purity) are pleasing (təhôrîm nōʿam) to him — the same nāʿam (pleasantness) used for Wisdom's paths in Proverbs 3:17. The cultic and ethical are fused: purity of speech is liturgically acceptable.
27: בּוֹצֵעַ בָּצַע (one who cuts/gains unjust gain) troubles (ʿōkēr) his household — the same verb used for Achan's troubling of Israel in Joshua 7:25. Śōnēʾ mattānôt (hater of bribes) will live — not merely "survives" but yiḥyeh (will have life), the full life that wisdom promises. The ethical and the vital are inseparably linked.
28: לֵב צַדִּיק (heart of the righteous) יֶהְגֶּה (meditates/weighs carefully) before answering — hāgâ is the meditation verb of Psalms 1:2 (meditating on Torah). The speech of the righteous is the outcome of inner deliberation. The mouth of the wicked (pî rəšāʿîm) yabbîaʿ (gushes, same as Proverbs 15:2) evil things — unfiltered overflow without prior thought.
29: רָחוֹק יהוה מֵרְשָׁעִים (the LORD is far from the wicked) — rāḥôq (distant) as a spatial metaphor for relational absence. This is not merely metaphor: in wisdom theology, the distance is the consequence of choosing the path that diverges from God. He hears (yišmaʿ) the prayer of the righteous — the covenant hearing-response formula; prayer reaches God because proximity exists.
30: מְאוֹר-עֵינַיִם (light of eyes, bright eyes) makes the heart glad (yəśammah-lēb) — a psychosomatic chain running from the visual sense to the emotional center. Good news (šəmûʿâ ṭôbâ) refreshes (tədaššen, causes to be fat/prosperous) bone — dāšan means to become fat or well-nourished; the idiom suggests deep physical restoration from within.
31: אֹזֶן שֹׁמַעַת תּוֹכַחַת חַיִּים (ear that listens to life-giving correction) — tôkaḥat ḥayyîm (reproof of life, life-giving reproof): the correction that leads to life is so described. The one who heeds it bəqereb ḥăkāmîm tālin (lodges/dwells among the wise) — lîn is an overnight lodging verb; the correction-hearer makes a permanent home in the company of wisdom.
32: פּוֹרֵעַ מוּסָר (one who neglects/throws off discipline) מוֹאֵס נַפְשׁוֹ (despises his own nephesh) — the person who refuses correction injures the very self they meant to protect. By contrast, šōmēaʿ tôkaḥat qōneh lēb (whoever listens to reproof gains/acquires heart/understanding) — qānâ is the acquisition verb used for buying wisdom (Proverbs 4:5, 7); correction is a purchase that yields understanding.
33: יִרְאַת יהוה מוּסַר חָכְמָה (fear of the LORD is training/discipline for wisdom) — mûsar (discipline/instruction) is the same word that opens ch. Proverbs 1:2; it closes Proverbs 15 to form a structural bracket. Wisdom is not innate but acquired through the fear-shaped formative process. Liphney kābôd ʿănāwâ (before honor is humility) — ʿănāwâ (humility, meekness) precedes honor; the sequence matters.
